The picture is of Oom the Omnipotent aka Pierre Bernard: sex guru, successful businessman, genuine scholar of Sanskrit teachings, shameless con artist, and also the man who introduced yoga to the American public. If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 542,396th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.0 of The Magic Monday FAQ here.

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use the button below to access my online tip jar.

comments.title: Abrahamic religions and astrology
comments.content:
Hey JMG,

Last week someone asked you about the different effects Muslims, Jews, and Christians experience as a result of hosting their public gatherings on Friday, Saturday, and Sunday respectively. You said that each were Venutian, Saturnine, and Solar, in both positive and negative senses, respectively.

Could you give a brief description of how you see these positives and negatives seem to manifest within each tradition/it’s correspondence to the planetary days?

Fascinating to think about, but being almost wholly ignorant of astrology, I could only guess based on googling the different planetary correspondences and hope what I was reading wasn’t just internet fluff that lacked any real understanding (which I would have an impossible time discerning).

replies.title: Re: Abrahamic religions and astrology
replies.content:
I can speak to Christianity and Judaism in this context, as I've attended services of both faiths (and in the case of Christianity, of many different denominations) and know a great many people in both. Islam I'll have to leave for others; of all the major world religions, it's the one that interests me least, and my readings in Muslim literature (including the Qu'ran, which I've read several times) have not encouraged me to look further, so I'll let someone else characterize it.

The sun as an astrological influence is expansive, generous, charitable; it's also arrogant and pushy. A strongly solar character is the kind of person you can't ignore; when he arrives on the scene, he becomes the center of attention at once, though this can be either because of the strength of his personality or the rudeness of his manners. He does everything on the grand scale; his virtues are as gargantuan as his vices, and the one thing he can't stand is when people don't take him with the seriousness that he thinks he deserves. That's Christianity in a nutshell.

Saturn as an astrological influence is fussy, precise, and traditional, with one eye always turned to the past. It never yields on the things that matter. A strongly Saturnine character is the kind of person who's always there, filling a familiar role, often all but invisible to those who aren't paying attention. In the Saturnine mind there's always close attention to the realities of existence, but also a pervasive habit of being self-centered that's at the other extreme from the solar mode, because it isn't interested in what others think. That's Judaism in a nutshell.

(Please note that these aren't characterizations of individual Christians or Jews, or even of those mythical critters, the average Christian or the average Jew. It's the religion itself as a collective personality that I'm talking about here -- and, of course, from an outsider's perspective.)

replies.title: Re: Abrahamic religions and astrology
replies.content:
Interestingly, Daoist horoscopy in medieval China assigned the Sun to Daoism and the Moon to Buddhism. Saturn is associated with longevity practices (see p.46 here).

https://archive.org/details/spp282_Indo-Iranian_Astrology_China/page/n47

An interesting parallel is in the Picatrix (III.1; 93–95), which states that the Sun rules over those who pray to the spirits of the planets (spirituum planetarum oratores). The Moon rules over those religions pray to idols and images (idola orantes et ymagines). (See Greer and Warnock 2010–2011: 135–137 for English translation).

replies.title: Re: Abrahamic religions and astrology
replies.content:
Fascinating! Explains a lot -- especially the obviously Venerean/Venereal personality of Islam. As an Islamicist working on the early modern Persianate world, I've been trying to find a way to explain the dramatic parting of cultural ways between the Persianate and Latinate spheres, despite their shared Greco-Arabic DNA. In particular, why does Christian Empire act so differently from Islamic Empire, the former often radically chauvinist and the latter often radically ecumenical, and extremely devoted to creating physical models of paradise -- complete with rivers of wine! -- on earth.

Even in the so-called classical period of Islam, when Arabic culture was still dominant, the most striking difference, much touted by colonialists and orientalists, was the sensuousness of Islamic societies in comparison to Christian, where puritanism flourished to a far greater degree. We're talking serious sex, drugs and rock n' roll, epitomized by the central social institution of the literary salon (majlis), where wine and homoeroticism were both celebrated in poetry and freely enjoyed in person. And with the arrival of the Turko-Mongol Perso-Islamic synthesis, which defined much of the vast Islamicate world down to and through the colonial period, the imperial Persian-cum-steppe nomad ethos of "feasting and fighting" was simply how empire was done. (A majority of Turko-Mongol rulers died either by alcoholism or fratricide.) Early modern Persianate culture more broadly -- encompassing a good third of the human race at the time -- is defined by wine and nature poetry (think Hafiz), love mysticism (often homoerotic, and conceiving of death as a wedding), punk-rock sufism (complete with wine, drugs, tattoos and piercings), the construction of garden cities mathematically modeling paradise (Shiraz, Isfahan, Agra), and of course the ubiquity of magic. Jealous Christian polemicists have long been and continue to be obsessed with such "Islamic decadence," epitomized by the trope of Muhammad as lecher.

(After all, why is "Venereal," alone among the planetary adjectives, now used in English exclusively in a negative sense, if not for Christian discomfort with sexy Venus herself? Why don't we call mental illness "Mercurial disease," depression "Saturnian disease" or narcissism "Solar disease"?)

That same puritanism/Protestantism, of course, has now been projected onto Islam itself through the mechanisms of colonialism and reformism, which, tellingly, have sought to stamp out the Venereal character of Islam as historically lived. But now the Protestantization of Islam makes more sense as a historical process: it's the attempt -- doomed in the long run, naturally, though moderately successful at the moment -- to replace Venus with the Sun, or rather Saturn, in Islam's natal horoscope. As a case in point: to insist that alcohol is forbidden in Islam, as modern puritans do, is to render the majority of Islamic societies historically somehow un-Islamic, is to recast Islam as merely an illegitimate form of Saturnian Judaism. But Protestantism is by definition rabidly anti-historical and wildly reductionist; thus Islam must be reduced to Scripture and Law only, in order to amputate its historically more dominant Poetry and Booze, which is to say, its cultural mainstream -- a classic divide et impera strategy. Now the Quran and the Sharia are indeed quite Saturnian in tenor, as is only natural, given their textual and geographical stemming. So whence the rampantly Venereal personality of Islam itself? Why is the defining trope of Islamic spirituality precisely drunkenness?*

Speaking of natal horoscopes: Muhammad's birth is traditionally associated with the Saturn-Jupiter conjunction in Scorpio in August 571, with many surviving horoscopes to that effect, thus making him a world-changing Lord of Conjunction (sahib-qiran) whose advent marks the transition from the Air cycle to the Water cycle. (This is the Middle Conjunction as defined by Abu Ma'shar, occurring roughly every 240 years.) Despite its rather straitlaced and arid Abrahamic roots, then (the Quran being mostly biblical and Mecca and Medina being desert towns), Islam's elemental nature as a Water religion, under the sign of Scorpio, together with its choice of Freya's day for communal worship, ensured that it would historically evolve into the cult of love, beauty and pleasure that made Persianate culture in particular so different from Latinate, whose elemental nature is rather Fire -- hence all that burning of witches and their books on the inquisitorial pyre, a phenomenon totally unknown in Islam. The early modern imperial West as the alchemical marriage, lately dissolved, of Water and Fire!

All of which prompts a few questions for you, JMG:

1) To what extent can and should we factor in the horoscope of a given religion's founder, and does that indeed help impart a specific and enduring elemental nature to that religion? Thus Water for Venereal Islam, Fire for Solar Christianity, Earth for Saturnian Judaism, etc.

2) Do you know of any other historical attempts, successful or otherwise, to artificially change a religion's planetary and elemental personality, as with the colonialist-reformist Protestantization of Islam now afoot?

3) Is geography destiny in this case too? Islam in North America, where it arrived in the first place by way of the African slave trade, is a creature very different from Islam in Mauritania, in Egypt, in Iran, in Indonesia, etc. That is, as new sects calve off from a given religion, do they acquire different natures based on their own natal horoscopes, and evolve according to the archetypes connected to their places of birth?

4) I assume the elemental nature of the Religion of Progress is Air -- would you agree? If so, it's appropriate that we'll be properly starting the next Air age in 2020. And the current theological inanity of this neognostic religion aside, perhaps its macro-historical function can be seen as completing the Western Water-Earth-Fire Islamo-Judeo-Christian triad?

Sorry for the barrage, and thanks for the fascinating discussion, very helpful indeed!


PS -- In this comparative context, my preliminary meditation on the theme of early modern Persianate-Latinate divergence with respect to occult science and grimoire culture may be of interest: http://tinyurl.com/y5uy8h3h


* On this thesis see especially Shahab Ahmed, What Is Islam?, Princeton University Press, 2016.

replies.title: Re: Abrahamic religions and astrology
replies.content:
Puritanical reactions are a phenomenon with a long history in Dar al-Islam, going back to the late Rashidun and early Umayyad periods. From a conservative Islamic perspective, it could be argued that the examples of sensualism that you cite are a form of backsliding into Jahiliyya and a corruption of the original purity of Islam as revealed by the Prophet Muhammed in the Quran and the Hadith. In Islamic history, periods of libertinism and hedonism are often followed by a puritanical backlash, with recent movements like the Wahhabis and Salafis being modern examples. Earlier examples would include the Kharijites and Ibn Khaldun’s denunciations of what he believed was the rampant immorality and backsliding of his time. So the notion that puritanical and reactionary movements in Islam are a product of Western imperialism and colonialism appears to be contradicted by Islamic history itself.

It makes me wonder if perhaps these puritanical movements that periodically surface in the Islamic world are a part of the shadow side of the Venusian element in Islam, a natural corrective factor that surfaces when the hedonistic tendencies get taken too far. It’s worth pointing out that Oswald Spengler argued in The Decline of the West that Islam has always had a puritanical element to it because it originated as a reaction to the decadent atmosphere of late pagan Mecca. Indeed, Spengler saw early Islam as the Magian counterpart of the Puritans and Calvinists of 16th and 17th century Europe. It has been noted by other scholars that Muhammed and early Islam were heavily influenced by Judaism and Gnostic Christianity, both of which have a strong Saturnine element to them, as did the Puritan and Calvinist movements in Europe, so perhaps there is also a Saturnian influence in Islam that is in competition with the Venusian element.

It occurs to me that another way the shadow side of Venus sometimes manifests itself is through blood sacrifice and sexual violence, something that has been well documented in academic research. We can see both of those at work in sects like Daesh and Boko Haram. We know from archaeological evidence that human sacrifice was extremely common in the goddess centered cultures of the Neolithic and that cultures like the Aztecs of Mesoamerica and the Thuggee of India engaged in human sacrifice until comparatively recently. Indeed, the American philosopher, historian and mythologist John David Ebert has argued that the Aztecs were in many respects the last surviving Neolithic civilization, while the Thuggee were Kali worshippers for whom ritual murder was a holy sacrament. As the more patriarchal centered cultures of the Bronze and Iron ages arose, human sacrifice was gradually replaced first by animal sacrifice and then by symbolic forms of sacrifice that don’t involve the spilling of blood, such as the Eucharist.

We also know that sexual violence has often been intertwined with the practice of human sacrifice. One example I came across in a discussion of Neolithic religious rites was an enactment of the Sacred Marriage in which a young man and woman would have ritual intercourse and then were killed by the other participants. This was apparently a common practice during the Neolithic in parts of the ancient Near East and what Marija Gimbutas called “Old Europe”.

A more recent example is from a photo I saw that was originally posted on a Daesh Twitter account and reposted on a military related blog I follow that as an example of Daesh atrocities. The photo showed a young Yazidi woman who had been stripped and raped and then had her carotid artery punctured. The assailants were holding her above a large bowl and bleeding her out into the bowl as if she was an animal being slaughtered according to the dictates of halal. It was clearly an act of ritual human sacrifice. We have also seen quite a few instances of sexual violence being directed against non-Muslim women by criminal gangs in places like Sweden, Rotherham in England and Cologne, Germany by people who justify their actions by citing their interpretation of Islamic teachings.

So while Islam no doubt has a strong Venusian element to it, don’t forget that Venus has a shadow side to it, and that shadow side sometimes manifests itself in very dark ways.

I meditated on this this past week, and so I'll expand a bit on what JMG said:

1. Another solar characteristic is centralized authority and hierarchy, which most sects of Christianity have in spades...think of the Catholic and Orthodox churches. Even the charismatic and Protestant versions still have very authoritative priests and ministers, for the most part, and gospel is, well, gospel. You don't deviate from that authority without being expelled from the church. Charisma is also a very solar quality and the evangelical sects of Christianity seem almost to deify charisma. Also, ahem, Jesus is the Son of God (I said this aloud and once I said it I couldn't unhear it. Of course it only works in English but still....)

2. A major theme of Saturn is limits and separation, which Judaism does constantly (separating light from darkness, the Sabbath from the other days of the week, Jews from the other nations, milk from meat, men from women, different kinds of fiber are forbidden to be sown in the same field/sewn into the same clothing, etc etc etc.) Positively, this separation is the fight against entropy and for order and justice in the world. But taken to an extreme, the obsession with separation and limits can become negative and destructive. Also in my meditations I remembered that the Jewish prayer for the renewal of the world and creation happens every 28 years...as it turns out, it's based on a (inaccurate medieval rabbi math) calculation of Saturn's orbit.

3. I don't know Islam as intimately as the other two. But thinking on it, Venus is the planet of peace, and the ideal state of the world according to Islam is that everyone is Muslim, submits to the will of God and is therefore at peace and happy. Throughout the Muslim world people greet each other Salaam aleikchem! which means "Peace be upon you!" The obsession with women's sexuality and its power (the extreme veiling of women is based on the idea that women's sexuality is active and destabilizing) is probably a vice of Venus. There is probably much more here that I'm missing...but these are just a few thoughts I had on the matter this past week.

-Ms. Krieger

Speaking as someone who'd really rather not, but understands the theory:

a) Some people take the view that, given that Christianity calls seemingly all spirits outside its purview 'demons' indiscriminately, we have no valid grounds for thinking these spirits are actually bad. However, pre-Christian folklore and more recent encounter reports sometimes agree with Christianity's assessment. Even presuming they're not evil, that doesn't mean they necessarily like humans (except with ketchup).

b) Others see things the way the Christian grimoire-users did, as did certain Neoplatonists before them: these are spirits who are in rebellion against the rightful order of the universe. You are making a citizen's arrest, forcing them to do socially useful community service, and thereby making the universe an incrementally better and more orderly place. Of course, framing it like that also highlights the problem: are you sure you're powerful enough to make that work? Freelance vigilantism works better in comics.

replies.title: Re: Huson
replies.content:
4) Huson's book is unusual in that, while he doesn't accept Wicca's origin story or ethics, he also doesn't let that stop him from incorporating aspects of Wicca that appealed to him. He treats it like a valid form of witchcraft, but not the witchcraft. He is extremely 'broad-church' and syncretic in the way that historical grimoire authors typically were and post-WWII writers on 'witchcraft' typically weren't.

Huson cheerfully combines Wiccan material with medieval Christian grimoires, rival witch-religions like Cochrane's Craft, and morally grey spellwork, three things orthodox Wiccan authors generally won't touch with a barge-pole. It all goes in the same stewpot. His book represents a moment before Wicca had much control over its own 'brand' and before it had semi-successfully laid claim to represent 'witchcraft.'

To be clear, I don't recommend the book except as a period piece. Some of the recipes are toxic and most of the rituals as written are metaphysically ill-advised (much like a medieval grimoire, really). However, if he does one thing right it's to reject the idea that 'witchcraft' means belonging to a religious faction.

His far more recent book on Tarot, on the other hand, I recommend heartily.

replies.content:
Mastering Witchcraft was one of the very first witchcraft books I bought and read when I took up witchcraft in the early 1970s. It is one of a handful of books from that period that I keep in my library and consult once in awhile.

I agree with Anonymous' description of the book. I recommend it with reservations to people who I think will get something out of it and be selective about what they do with it. There are a few bits in it which I incorporated into my personal practice right away and still use with minor modifications: the consecration prayer that begins "Emen Hetan", and the incense blends for summer and winter sabbats. I suspect that the latter recipes are Huson's own work, as he wrote another book called Mastering Herbalism which gives a lot of space to incense.

I agree that it is a period piece. For that reason alone it is valuable for people who are curious about what American witchcraft looked like before British Traditional Wicca and feminism became overridingly dominant influences upon it, especially since most of the primary sources from the pre-Internet era are either unavailable to the public or are small-circulation newsletters that latecomers can only find in archives. The very concept of a Magisterial Coven has been dropped right down the memory hole.

I have more interest in and respect for ceremonial magic now than I did then. If I knew a few experienced witches who had the time for it, I think it would be fun to form a coven using Mastering Witchcraft as our Book of Shadows and systematically work through everything he includes.

comments.title: Demon possession
comments.content:
Archdruid, I would greatly appreciate your help with something. It will require quite a bit of set-up, but there is a magical dimension so please bear with me. Another heads up for all readers, this will deal with some fairly dark stuff.

I suffered a severe trauma on multiple levels of my being at age 14 when I was given what was supposedly an acne drug, Roaccutane (branded as Accutane over in the States). Since then I have been, well... almost completely impotent and without libido, having been a perfectly sexually healthy young teenager beforehand. 11 years have now passed, with no improvement.

I've also had some terrible struggles with my mental health; I was admitted to a psych ward by my parents a few years back where I spent a number of days, but thankfully made it out without being subjected to further medical experimentation. I've always had a deep-seated fear of drugs of all kinds which I now think stemmed from an unconscious recognition of the damage Roaccutane did to my young body, mind and soul, although I only made the connection to my impotence and mental disturbances in late December of last year, so only a few months back.

To be clear, I should say that these are both now officially acknowledged (by the FDA) side effects of the drug. What happened in terms of my personal journey, I think, is that I reached a point of emotional readiness where I could finally begin to contemplate the terrible possibility that a drug had caused this damage to me, and a quick Google search revealed strong corroborating evidence in the form of the aforesaid official mea culpa and plenty of personal testimonies which match my own experience.

It didn't take me long after taking the drug to realise I was impotent, of course, but having suffered such a strange and horrific trauma at such a young age I was unable to make head or tails of it. In my naive young mind, this was a drug that was supposed to help me - and it did clear my acne - so how could it have caused me such harm? This was reinforced by doctors I spoke to over the years, who one and all denied outright that there could be any connection between the drug and my sexual issues when I raised the question tentatively. I supposed they instinctively felt they had to defend the profession on which they stake their livelihoods. I, for my part, was only too willing to take these so-called authorities at their word and accept their diagnosis that my problems were entirely psychological, even though this made no sense of my experience, which as I said, is one of near-total impotence, and led to an extreme mind-body disconnect.

Anyway, so I've been beginning to process all this in the last few months. But here's where I need your help. For many years I've had this recurring experience where when the weekend comes around I suddenly feel all the life sucked out of me. In some cases and especially lately this has been accompanied by a devastating sort of ego-death experience, where some cherished notion of who I am is shattered and I'm left feeling completely adrift, lost, and even, in the most extreme instances, as if I have no free will. This usually begins on Friday and peaks on Saturday night, then gradually heals over Sunday as a new ego sort of spontaneously stitches itself together for the week ahead. Needless to say, this is exhausting.

As I've been going through all this emotional processing lately, the thought has come to me that this experience may be a way of reliving the trauma of having been symbolically killed at a young age by this drug. You know, since on the cusp of adulthood I suddenly ran into this thing that stopped me dead in my tracks, likewise on the cusp of the weekend, when many young adults like myself go out to pubs, bars and clubs looking to meet sexual partners. Yet the experience is so severe and even with this insight remains utterly terrifying, particularly this sensation of having no free will.

Here's where magic and occultism come into the picture. About three years back, I had a mental breakdown, largely, I think, as a result of all the aforementioned stuff, the trauma from this drug. During the process of rebuilding, I came across your work and was fascinated. It seemed like a much more balanced approach to spirituality than the New Age schlock I was used to. So I began practising magic daily as per your coauthored Learning Ritual Magic. Only after completing the 9 month course did I discover that it's generally advised that anybody with mental health disturbances, particularly those on the end of the spectrum that tend to get labelled psychotic, avoid magic. I didn't like to think of myself as psychotic and still don't, to me it's such a poorly defined concept that in practice rarely amounts to more than a means of social control, enforcing an arbitrary standard of normal; no doubt many if not all people asking questions on here would be labelled psychotic if they expressed similar thoughts in a consultation room with a psychiatrist. And I certainly never dreamed of taking any of the poisons called anti-psychotic drugs, having done my due diligence on them. Still, I had to admit to myself I wasn't doing too well - I'd been on welfare for a couple of years, living with my parents, not really functioning. So I decided to stop the practice, with the proviso that I could come back to it if and when I was doing any better.

Fast forward to just two weeks ago. I've been back in full-time work for the last 6 months, off welfare, moved out into my own place again. So I decide, why not give this magic stuff a go again? I have to say, it never fully let go of its hold on me even when I wasn't practising. But, full disclosure, I was also motivated by an element of fear. I had come across a discussion on one of these Magic Mondays, which I occasionally browsed, about this idea that once you've metaphorically cracked out of the egg and recognised that you live in a magical universe, there's no way back and the only constructive way forward is to continue working with the tools of magic. To be honest, this put me into a bit of a spin. On the one hand, as somebody with a fair amount of mental disturbance, which even though I was functioning better I couldn't kid myself had entirely resolved, the consensus among occultists was clear: I should leave magic well alone. On the other hand, having already delved fairly deep into practice I was apparently obliged to continue?! I couldn't resolve this dilemma, and so I decided I would resume practice again and just see how it went.

Practice had been going quite well these last two weeks, and my ability to function seemed not in the least bit impeded, enhanced if anything. Then, this weekend came around, and that familiar feeling came over me again. I felt myself cracking, and my capacity for free will seemed to slip away. This came to a very disturbing head this Saturday when, after going to a bar to see some live music in an unsuccessful attempt to just have some fun, I found myself feeling so overwhelmed that I had to retreat back to my room at home. I sat on the bed head in hands, and here's where things started to turn really nightmarish. I started making this heavy breathing sound, almost like a choked sort of barking. It was just coming out of me, I felt I had no conscious control over it. Then I collapsed onto the bed and began reeling around. My arms flexed and waved themselves around, the hands contorted into claw shapes. I was now making grunting noises, very animalistic. This continued for several minutes and then, in a daze, I blew out the candle and went straight to sleep.

I honestly felt like I was possessed by a demon. Obviously, this terrifies me and I don't have a clue what to do about it. Should I stop all magic immediately? Should I seek help from some kind of healer? Or is it something I will just have to adjust to, somehow?

I'm wondering whether this demon has been living inside me for all these years and I'm only now recognising it as such. As I say, this periodic experience of losing my free will has been there for a long time, possibly stretching back to when I took the acne drug. I'm wondering whether the demon is in fact some kind of manifestation of the spirit of that drug? Is this kind of thing even possible? The demon seems kind of sickly, emaciated and foul-smelling.

At the very least, it doesn't seem to have any intent to harm other people. Its main concern seems to be causing me to suffer and repeatedly experience failure.

Any advice would be so welcome. I guess it's good if I'm recognising something that has probably been the case for a good long while. You know, the idea that I'm better off knowing than not. One positive that immediately comes to mind is I no longer have to chastise myself with, "why do I feel that I don't have free will? What's wrong with me?" - instead I can say, "there really is something constraining my free will." But still... this is very upsetting and confusing. I did a few Tarot readings this morning looking for guidance and they seemed to suggest that if I gave up on magic the demon would go quiet and I could live some sort of life on the straight and narrow. Yet I can't help the feeling this would be sort of like lobotomising myself, shutting off my awareness to realms I know are real. Conversely, when I asked the Tarot what would happen if I continued with magic the answer seemed to be that no matter how much dedication I put into my practice, the demon would still have my number, indeed even if I developed great magical skill I would paradoxically remain locked in a state of helplessness. The final card in the reading, however, suggested some kind of resolution might be possible, not directly through the practices of ritual magic, but through karmic retribution. I don't know what that would look like exactly, but that's the message I took.

Or am I just a basketcase? It would be more comforting to believe that in a way... But I don't want to go back on welfare and be dependent on the state (and my parents) all my life, if I can avoid it. Neither of them are particularly dependable, anyway, so who knows how long that would even be an option.

Ugh, I feel like I've landed myself in a terrible spot. As I say, a little guidance would be very welcome, from anyone. I don't really know where else to turn than here... I'm acutely aware that the wrong word said to the wrong person and I could be wheeled straight back to the loony bin. And I might have a harder time talking my way out the second time.

There is a group of Spiritualists based near where I've just moved to, who, whether coincidentally or otherwise, I discovered on Friday, the day before this (quasi-)demonic encounter - could they be worth talking to, or is there a risk they could make things far worse? I have no idea how competent they are or even exactly what they do. I spoke to one of them on the phone to try to find out a bit about them and she talked a lot about 'healing' (not in direct relation to me, I should add - I didn't disclose much about myself) as well as 'clairvoyance', and seemed a friendly, likeable sort. But yesterday I came across a passage in Regardie's Tree of Life where he argues that all Spiritualism is dangerous and debased, working haphazardly with beings in the Lower Astral. Any thoughts, anyone?

I realise I may be asking a lot already, but in addition to general guidance, I'd also be interested in anyone's thoughts about how demons relate to mental illness more broadly. All this has me wondering, is mental illness often demonic? I'm vaguely aware that in certain cultures which have little concept of mental illness have well-defined concept of demon-possession that covers much of the same ground, although I can't refer to a source on that so maybe I'm skewing it somewhat.

As a side note, I followed the discussion a few weeks back about Pluto with great interest. I was born in 92, while Pluto was in Scorpio, and my experience with Roaccutane seems like a classic Plutonian encounter, at least as I understand it. A drug which is routinely prescribed to young, healthy kids for nothing more than teenage acne, that causes literally unspeakable damage, which medical authorities unanimously denied until only a couple of years ago. There are so many layers of deceit and denial here which I'm only just coming to grips with, right down to suppressed clinical data and deliberate obfuscation of the origin and true nature of the drug (it's a chemotherapeutic, originally introduced for skin cancer, yet deemed too dangerous even for that! Before, of course, being rebranded as a 'vitamin A derivative' and marketed to healthy children for pimples.)

I'm including a sketch of the demon I made the morning after the incident, to try to better understand its nature.

[IMAGE:0]

Thanks.

replies.title: Re: Demon possession
replies.content:
Okay, first, you need to leave magic strictly alone from here on in -- as in, for the rest of your life. This sort of thing is the reason why. You're never going to be able to shut it away completely, but that's not a reason to keep practicing something that could land you in an institution permanently.

Second, what you've described doesn't sound at all like demonic possession in the proper sense of the word, but it's quite possible that what you've experienced is the spirit or egregor of the drug.

Third, if alternative health care modalities are legal in the jurisdiction in which you live, you might consider looking into them. These days, MDs are basically shills for the pharmaceutical industry, and it's very unlikely that they'll be willing to take your condition seriously, much less do anything to help. Alternative health care practitioners are another matter. If traditional Chinese medicine (TCM) is legal to practice where you live, in particular, a competent practitioner might be able to do quite a bit to help you.

Fourth, spiritualism is a very, very mixed bag. Some Spiritualist groups are capable and honest; others are neither. Of course the same thing is true of other religious traditions!

You're in a very tough spot -- one of many, many people who've suffered harm from an out-of-control medical industry that forgot a very long time ago that "First do no harm" is the first line of the Hippocratic oath. There will doubtless be some kind of karmic issue involved, but you don't have to worry about that; stop all magical practices, get rid of the books, find some other activity to occupy your thoughts and spare time, see what you can do about getting effective alternative health care, and you can go on to have a better life.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: It really does depend from person to person. Some people, when they take up scrying, get insights into themselves first off; others get other things. What I'd encourage you to do is to take your next scrying and, in the daily meditations afterward, approach it from a different point of view every day. Let's say you've done a scrying of Air; put one meditation (at least) into seeing what kind of sense it makes if it symbolizes something about your own habits of thinking; put another into seeing how much sense it makes if it's about mind in general; and go on from there, seeing how many different things you can think of that air can mean, and trying to interpret the scrying from each of those. Don't be surprised if it makes sense in several ways!

Hello! I have a few questions:

Firstly, if someone is a nasty, selfish person as a young person and changes into a more likeable, altruistic person as he ages, does that mean that this will lead him or her into a total different direction in his or her next incarnation, compard what would have happened if said person had remained nasty and selfish?

Thirdly, how specific is the transmission of character from one incarnation to the next? For example, if someone doesn't like cheap and shoddy goods and has a taste for good quality, useful items, does this specific trait carry over? Or, for another example, an inability to bear and handle superficiality in relating to other people?

Fourthly, if one has already set a schedule for a long series of meditations about a subject, like the Oghams, for example, how best to accommodate meditating about the other things worth meditating over that come up from time to time?

Thanks!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) That's the whole point of incarnation; you have the chance to change your trajectory, with results that will reshape your future lioves.

2) Quite the contrary. You achieve Gwynfydd, in an important sense, while living, and when death comes it's simply a matter of shedding bodies that are no longer necessary to you, and stepping into a realm you've perceived dimly for some time already. There's a reason why so many people have commented that being around a holy person feels like being in another, more sacred realm.

3) It depends very much on the individual, and on such details as why you have a dislike of shoddy things.

4) There are various ways to do it. One that works well for me is to keep track of subjects for meditation, using a blank book or notebook, and save them for when the long series of meditations is over.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
There is indeed a standard Druid prayer, the Universal Druid Prayer. It appears in several forms; the one most often used in AODA runs like this:

Grant, O Holy Ones, thy protection
And in protection, strength;
And in strength, understanding;
And in understanding, knowledge;
And in knowledge, the knowledge of justice:
And in the knowledge of justice, the love of it;
And in that love, the love of all existences;
And in the love of all existences, the love of Earth our mother and all goodness.

And yes, it's a good intro to any sort of communion with the deities.

replies.title: Re: Age of Aquarius
replies.content:
One of the things I've noticed over the years is that when there's a major change in the works, most people are aware of it on a deep subconscious level. When it bubbles up to the surface, though, it takes on the trappings of the person's culture, convictions, beliefs and so forth, frequently colored by other people who are saying the same thing. A lot of Christians are interpreting it as the Rapture. There are, or were, other groups looking for friendly aliens to bail us out, or intervention by a Higher Power, or whatever.

Clearly, something is going on. Equally clearly, a lot of the people talking about it don't really have a good grip on it, and are trying to cram a large triangular peg into a small pentagonal hole. Another thing is reasonably clear: anyone who thinks we're headed for everything turning out good, great, glorious and so forth is dreaming. This level of shift is going to solve a lot of things that have been problems for the last couple of thousand years - many of which many of us don't regard as problems at all - but it's going to bring its own set of challenges - most of which we simply don't have a clue about.

"Business as usual" isn't going to survive, but exactly what's included in that phrase isn't at all clear. The next few decades are going to be rough, and I doubt most people are at all prepared.

John Roth

comments.title: Deliberate Practice
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

Researchers who study top performing athletes, musicians, and other elite-level performers have a concept known as "deliberate practice". The idea is that by practicing certain skills with a very specific goal, the practice time becomes very effective in advancing skills. In other words, by using *deliberate* practice, practice makes perfect, not practice makes permanent.

I have been thinking about this relative to my daily spiritual practice of SOP, discursive meditation, and divination. While step #1 is just to put in the time on a daily basis, which I am doing, I suspect that I am just going through the routine on some levels and could be getting a lot more out of the time if I could be more systematically effective.

The problem is, when we are talking about spiritual practice, it is not like tennis or piano which can be objectively measured and subject to performance metrics. Two comments of yours I have really appreciated were 1) the 3-card daily Tarot spread which gets reviewed on a daily basis to create a sort of feedback loop which should lead to increasing accuracy and 2) suggestions to break down parts of the SOP, such as visualization, by practicing visualization exercises.

Sorry if this question seems slightly absurd, because the whole idea of "measuring" spiritual work is obviously not the point. Checklists, conceptual frameworks, and step-by-step guides are only going to apply to a limited extent. On the other hand, this type of work is serious business--possibly the most important that there is--so any suggestions you have along these lines would be greatly appreciated.

replies.title: Re: Deliberate Practice
replies.content: It's not hard at all to use the same principle in magic. You can work on maintaining mental focus and concentration; you can work on building clear imagery, and on bringing in other sense modalities, so that you feel and hear and smell as well as see the things you imagine; you can work on smoothness and precision of movement; you can pay close attention to the effect you get and work on intensifying it. In my experience, it's best to attend to one of those intensively at a time -- say, a week of hard work on one, then a week of just doing the practice, then a week of hard work on another, rinse and repeat and before long you're really getting good.

comments.title: Electro-magnetic radiation
comments.content:
I've been following with some interest the discussion about putting sharp iron on wifi routers to diminish the addictive glamour of the internet. I've done it in my house and the results are ambiguous, though it certainly hasn't hurt.

I'm curious, JMG, what your take is on the effects of electro-magnetic radiation from wifi, cell phone networks...etc. I find it very likely that it has deleterious health effects as many argue (to what degree I don't know, it seems to vary person to person like many other environmental influences on health). I'm curious what a magical worldview would have to say about the meaning and significance of these energies. How do they effect the etheric, astral, and spiritual as well as physical bodies? And what can one do to limit impact?

replies.title: Re: Electro-magnetic radiation
replies.content:
Speaking only from my personal experience here, EM signals (wifi, cell phones, bluetooth, GPS) are harmful and painful. I've known for a number of years that I can perceive these things, and that I experience them more acutely based on three variables: strength of the signal; distance to the source; and the amount of data being transmitted. The first two of these should be straightforward; the last means (roughly) that the very same wifi router, at the same distance, hurts quite a lot more when lots of people are streaming video then when no one is actually connected to it, and it's just occasionally pinging the room. (On multiple occasions, I've used this trick to figure out that a room I expected to be empty was actually occupied by people on computers. Neat, huh?)

According to my doctor, my condition is "electromagnetic hypersensitivity syndrome," and the appropriate treatment is simply to stay away from such things as much as possible. (While I appreciate the validation, I also knew that already. Sigh.) If I've been exposed to a signal that was particularly intense, or even to a more moderate signal over a long duration, it can take some time before things settle back down to their baseline (much the way that your ears keep ringing even after a loud noise has stopped, or the way it takes some minutes to really warm up, even after you've come in from the cold). There also seem to be certain frequencies that I pick up far more intensely than others; that may vary from sufferer to sufferer.

From a magical perspective, I think part of this may be happening at the etheric level. Main reason for this: assuming that I'm no longer being exposed to an especially painful signal (e.g., it happened all afternoon at work, but now I've just come home), a long shower seems to work wonders for helping me to "reset" more quickly than I otherwise would.

"Grounding" on the earth--again, in a relatively safe, low-EM place--is also quite helpful for me. Bare feet are good, laying full out on the ground for 10-15 minutes is even better.

What else can we do to limit exposure?

Stop buying "smart" devices, and disable the various signals on any you may already have. Fitbits, in my experience, are especially painful, since they are *constantly* sending signals, on one of the frequencies that I feel most intensely. Also note that "updates" have a way of re-enabling wireless communications. Check and re-check your settings often.

Disable your wireless router, and connect with a physical cable instead (which will also increase your connection speed).

Petition the appropriate persons to forbid this 5G nonsense, which the media has been shrilling trumpeting as inevitable. A recent article in the BBC informed the world that we will need approximiately 70 times the number of cell towers that we currently have, in order to make 5G happen. In the US, the FCC plans to waive even the minimal permitting/siting requirements that we currently have, in order to make all those towers possible.

You may also want to re-post this query on JMG's other blog on the next open post; there's a fellow sufferer who posts there somewhat regularly, but I'm not sure if she reads this blog.

replies.title: Re: Electro-magnetic radiation
replies.content: We are not smartphone people, so have no use for wifi in the house. But we do have a router (separate from our modem), which we hook up sometimes for the convenience of visitors. I don't always remember to unhook it later. However, when I notice that I've been sleeping poorly this is the first thing I check. More often than not, after a spell of lousy sleep, I disconnect the router and things improve immediately. I still wonder if this effect is real, or  just a psychological boost for having *done* something.

replies.content:
Others may also be waking up to these dangers, as evidenced by a recent article in (of all places) Wired magazine: https://www.wired.com/story/why-5g-makes-reconsider-health-effects-cellphones/

Of course, it has now been drastically cut down by the editors; see the note at the top. Original version here: https://web.archive.org/web/20190401150103/https://www.wired.com/story/why-5g-makes-reconsider-health-effects-cellphones/

One particularly interesting paragraph from the original, much longer, unedited version of that piece:

"It turns out that sweat glands, right under the skin, effectively act as antennas in response to the very-high-frequency millimeter waves planned to be used in 5G communications—which is why the Department of Defense uses millimeter-wave crowd-control guns. If you're hit by one of these beams, it apparently feels as if your body is on fire. But there’s no lasting harm, according to DOD."

I thought this was supposed to be clearer than tarot? ;-)

replies.title: Re: Checking geomancy interp
replies.content:
If I may, this chart says three things to me:
(1) You've already made up your mind (that's Cauda Draconis in the 1st);
(2) you should in fact do it (Conjunctio as Judge); and
(3) you'll be glad you did (Laetitia as Sentence [16th figure = Judge + 1st Mother]).

To my mind, Conjunctio straightforwardly both avows (gives a direct answer) and affirms (says "yes" to) the query, saying essentially "yes, you should come together with others to make this thing happen." (By contrast, Carcer as Judge would avow but deny, giving the direct answer of "no, you should stay clear." The other six possible Judges would disavow, thereby giving a more complicated answer.)

FWIW, I see Cauda in the Right Witness as a kind of "call back" to Cauda in the 1st. Reading the Court as a whole, we find the choice you've already made to take on the leadership role being the right one, bringing good results for your son and/or the organization, and leaving you glad of your choice afterward.

JMG, if you have anything else to say about the Right Witness in particular, I'd be very glad to hear from you and learn!

replies.title: Re: Checking geomancy interp
replies.content:
Okay, first of all the thing that gets most beginners confused about geomantic readings is that they forget that every figure doesn't get equal weight. Your judge, as modified by the witnesses, is the answer to your question. Cauda Draconis as your right witness says that you've already made up your mind, Conjunctio as the judge says that you're going to do it, Laetitia as the left witness says that it'll work out well. That's your answer.

Everything else is at most a minor commentary on those three figures. Until you've gotten good at reading the Judge and Witnesses, in fact, I recommend ignoring the rest of the chart completely until you've reached the point where you can glance at the Witnesses and Judge and snap out the answer without having to think about it. Then you'll be able to give the other figures the very modest role they deserve in your readings.

comments.title: Baths
comments.content:
A couple of questions about bathing practices.

1. For the last month or so I've been doing the basic cold water bath you've recommended. Every morning, I fill the bathroom sink with cold water and wash down my body. Time permitting, I let myself air dry before taking a regular shower with soap. After the first few days I started adding salt to the water. Then a week later I got the idea to also light a candle and some incense, and shortly thereafter it occurred to me to bless the candle, incense, water and salt in succession, calling upon a trinity of Celtic deities in each case and blessing with a triqueta drawn in the air in the appropriate color, vibrating the divine names, and also asking for Spirit to be present in the room with a vibrated AWEN. In the case of the lower 4 elements, I often ask for a specific blessing-- "May this fire purify my will" "May this incense purify my thoughts" "May this water purify my feelings or energy" "May this salt purify my body." I've been calling this my Elemental Bath. Any thoughts?

2. Once a week, I've been doing this, but adding a cup of vinegar and teaspoon of cayenne to create a hoodoo bath. This seems to be very helpful. A couple of days ago I did the hoodoo bath, but I wasn't paying attention to what I was doing, and I let the water go down the drain. I always do this with the salt water, but with the vinegar and cayenne I take a potful out back and throw it on our big avocado tree. The effect of letting the water go down the drain was... interesting. It felt somehow energetically WRONG, and even kind of nauseating. The next few days were unpleasant; I felt weird and sluggish, the way I used to when I would do complicated qigong forms in energetically toxic environments. Today I re-did the bath properly, with the tree, though when I took the water out back I got the impression that the (much smaller) orange tree would like some as well. The weird feeling isn't gone, but pouring the water out back felt much better-- the orange tree is blossoming, and I felt a sense of connecting with the sweet energy of orange blossoms.

I've been thinking about what's going on here, and I'd like your thoughts. We know just from studying the physical world that trees take our waste and turn it into nutrients; we assume in magic that this happens on the "energetic" level as well. It seems like, by giving the waste water to a tree in the hoodoo bath, we create a kind of circuit-- not only is negative energy removed, but we give it as a gift to another being, which then returns it to us in purified form. By letting it go down the drain, this circuit was unable to be completed.... except that in another, strange sense, it did seem to create a circuit-- only this time, between me and the weird underworld of the city's sewer system! Hence, I think, the weird and nauseating feeling, and the sense of energetic weirdness that followed over the next few days. What do you think-- Am I right in my interpretation? And-- I know the instructions for the bath are "pour the water onto the biggest and strongest tree you can find" but I got the very strong sense that the (smaller, weaker) orange tree would really like some. Would you have paid attention to a feeling like this, or does it disrupt the effects of the bath?

replies.title: Re: Baths
replies.content:
1) That sounds like a worthwhile experiment.

2) I'd pay attention to a feeling like that. The orange tree may well have been asking for a share, and human energies are as beneficial to trees as tree energies are to humans, so why not?

comments.title: Do you know of any herbs that help open the third eye?

replies.title: Re: Do you know of any herbs that help open the third eye?
replies.content:
No, and I don't recommend using herbs for that purpose. If you learn to walk with crutches you may never learn to walk without them.

The pineal gland is of central importance in quite a few systems of occult training, including the one I wrote about in The Druid Magic Handbook. The calcification of the pineal gland is a normal part of maturation, and is one of the things that makes puberty happen -- an uncalcified pineal gland produces secretions that keep the genitals from developing. If the people who are trying to uncalcify their pineal glands were to succeed, I don't think they'd be happy about the impact on their sex lives...

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: What’s the Druid view of sin?  Most religions have a pretty similar view of sin—murder, adultery, dissing God, etc—is that also true of druidry?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I’m neither JMG nor a Druid, but If I may, I'd like to suggest that “sin” is a Abrahamic term of art that means more than simply “doing wrong.” The concept of sin is tied into the concept of the world, and human nature, as fallen from a state of grace, and from a communion with God. This unless you as a Druid also follow an Abrahamic faith (which has quite a respectable pedigree), it doesn’t apply.

Other traditions certainly acknowledge that one can act wrongly in various ways—one can be unjust, intemperate, imprudent, cruel, selfish, etc.—but this is not because there is something wrong with either human nature or the world. It’s just that you have the capacity to make both good choices and bad choices.

As for which choices are good and bad, it’s cliche but I’d suggest looking around you, at the real-world consequences of various choices once they’ve solidified as habit and then character. What kind of character and life do you want?

Oh, and ignore the Donald Trumps and Jeff Bezos and other benefactors of survivorship bias; their lives are unrepresentative and thus a poor guide for yours. And, obviously, think about what you really value. Don’t just romanticize “success” as conventionally defined.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: That's like asking about the Druid view of the Crucifixion; we don't have one. Outside the Abrahamic faiths, which obsessively conflate them, spirituality is not the same thing as morality; if you want to understand the ethical implications of something, take up the study of ethical philosophy; if you want to get into spirituality, then you can talk to a Druid.

replies.title: Re: I need some counsel organizing my magical practice.
replies.content: Okay, you're way overdoing it. You've loaded yourself with way too much, and that way lies failure. Cut way back -- if necessary, just to the LBRP and Middle Pillar every day -- and do that until you're comfortable with it and have no trouble getting it done every day. If that's easy, add meditation; if both those are easy, add divination. Then, when you've got a nice regular routine of practice that you can maintain without any effort, add in *one* special practice once a week. Only when you can do that easily should you begin to think about adding more.

comments.title: Astrology/out of bounds planets
comments.content:
You've wrote about the importance of parallels, I'm curious what your take on 'out of bounds' planets are in a natal chart.

Out of bounds Uranus for example. Does it effect the interpretation of Uranus in the chart? If so, is it a small or large effect do you think?

replies.title: Re: Astrology/out of bounds planets
replies.content: I haven't found "out of bounds" planets to have anything like the sort of effect that some modern astrologers claim for them; still, I've only been working with declinations for a couple of years now, and we'll see what I think when I have more experience with them.
